returns again to the archway of the Asoka grove

Canto XLVI

Having killed five more generals sent by Ravana, Hanuman
returns again to the archway of the Asoka grove

__data__.text: Ravana, doubting Hanuman's true nature and strength, dispatched five powerful generals – Virupaksa, Yupaksa, Durdhara, Praghasa, and Bhasakarna – to capture him.  He reminded them of their army's past victories but cautioned against underestimating Hanuman.  Ravana stressed the importance of strategic action and self-preservation, acknowledging the unpredictable nature of battle. The generals, energized by their orders, set off with their troops and diverse weaponry.  They found Hanuman radiating a brilliant light, possessing immense strength, agility, and intelligence. They immediately attacked him with various weapons.  Durdhara struck Hanuman's head with five arrows, which had little effect and merely resembled lily petals adorning him.  Enraged, Hanuman leaped skyward with a mighty roar that echoed in all directions.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XLVI

Having killed five more generals sent by Ravana, Hanuman
returns again to the archway of the Asoka grove

Canto XLVI

Having killed five more generals sent by Ravana, Hanuman
returns again to the archway of the Asoka grove

__data__.text: Hanuman continued to fend off Ravana's generals.  Durdhara attacked Hanuman with a barrage of arrows, but Hanuman deflected them with his roar, much like a wind dispersing a monsoon cloud.  Annoyed, Hanuman grew in size and landed on Durdhara's chariot with tremendous force, destroying it and killing the general.  Seeing this, Virupaksa and Yupaksa attacked Hanuman in the air with clubs, but Hanuman countered their attack and descended swiftly to the ground.  He then uprooted a sal tree and used it to kill both Virupaksa and Yupaksa.  Upon witnessing the deaths of these three generals, Praghasa and Bhasakarna approached Hanuman, one armed with a spear and the other with a dart, and attacked him.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XLVI

Having killed five more generals sent by Ravana, Hanuman
returns again to the archway of the Asoka grove

Canto XLVI

Having killed five more generals sent by Ravana, Hanuman
returns again to the archway of the Asoka grove

__data__.text: Hanuman, wounded but enraged, retaliated fiercely against Ravana's forces. He uprooted a mountain peak, complete with its flora and fauna, and used it to crush the generals Virupaksa and Yupaksa.  With the five generals defeated, Hanuman proceeded to decimate the remaining army. He employed a unique battle tactic, using their own resources against them – horses against horses, elephants against elephants, warriors against warriors, and chariots against chariots. The battlefield became littered with the bodies of fallen soldiers, animals, and broken chariots, blocking all pathways.  Victorious, Hanuman stood on the archway, poised and ready for the next challenge, much like time itself awaiting the destruction of all beings.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XLVII

Prince Aksa, son of Ravana, who came to meet
Hanuman in combat, is killed

Canto XLVII

Prince Aksa, son of Ravana, who came to meet
Hanuman in combat, is killed

__data__.text: Hanuman's devastating attack left Ravana's five generals and their armies destroyed.  Ravana turned his attention to his son, Prince Aksa, who eagerly awaited battle.  Inspired by his father's gaze, Aksa, armed with a magnificent bow, sprang forward, ready for combat. He mounted a radiant chariot, adorned with gold, and charged towards Hanuman. This chariot, earned through rigorous austerities, was a formidable war machine, equipped with flags, a jeweled ensign, swift horses, and an array of weapons.  Aksa, a warrior of divine prowess, filled the battlefield with the sounds of his advancing army. He approached Hanuman, who stood defiantly on an archway.  Hanuman, poised for battle, was surprised by the young prince's challenge.  Aksa, confident and strong, considered Hanuman's power and his own might.  Fueled by anger at Hanuman's strength, Aksa firmly positioned himself and shot three arrows at Hanuman, provoking him to fight.  Aksa, adorned with gold ornaments, saw Hanuman's pride and readiness for battle and prepared to engage him in a duel that promised to be legendary, causing awe among gods and demons alike.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XLVII

Prince Aksa, son of Ravana, who came to meet
Hanuman in combat, is killed

Canto XLVII

Prince Aksa, son of Ravana, who came to meet
Hanuman in combat, is killed

__data__.text: The earth trembled, the sun dimmed, the wind stilled, and the very mountain shook as Hanuman and Aksa clashed.  Aksa, a skilled archer, shot three arrows, tipped with gold and resembling venomous serpents, into Hanuman's head.  Wounded and bleeding, Hanuman shone like the rising sun, the arrows in his forehead resembling its rays.  Seeing Aksa's magnificent chariot and weapons raised in challenge, Hanuman, the monkey general, rejoiced and grew in size, his fury burning like the sun. He glared at Aksa, his eyes blazing with anger.  Aksa, likened to a storm cloud, rained arrows upon Hanuman, who stood firm like a mountain.  Hanuman roared with delight at the challenge. Aksa, fueled by youthful pride, advanced on Hanuman, who watched him with a mixture of awe and apprehension. As Aksa's arrows found their mark, Hanuman roared, a sound like thunder, and stretched his limbs, preparing to retaliate. Aksa pursued the airborne Hanuman, showering him with arrows.  Hanuman, swift and agile, dodged the missiles, moving through the air like the wind. He paused, observing Aksa's skill and determination with respect, and considered his next move.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:

Prince Aksa, son of Ravana, who came to meet

Canto XLVII

Prince Aksa, son of Ravana, who came to meet
Hanuman in combat, is killed

__data__.text: Hanuman, wounded by Aksa's arrows, considered his opponent's skill. He observed Aksa's youthful prowess and the power he wielded, acknowledging that such strength could shake even gods and demons.  Realizing the growing threat, Hanuman decided he could not afford to underestimate Aksa.  He swiftly killed Aksa's eight chariot horses, causing the chariot to crash.  Aksa leaped from the wreckage, but Hanuman caught him by the legs, spun him around, and threw him to the ground with devastating force, leaving him broken and lifeless.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman, after slamming Aksa to the ground, instilled fear in Ravana.  The observing sages, celestial beings (Yakshas and Nagas), and gods led by Indra, watched in astonishment as Hanuman killed the prince.  The valiant Hanuman, having defeated Aksa, who rivaled Indra's son in battle and whose eyes were reddened, then returned to his position on the archway, awaiting another chance to vanquish more of Ravana's forces.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Ravana, grieving yet composed, commands his powerful son, Indrajit, to subdue Hanuman.  He praises Indrajit's skill with weapons, recalling his past victories against gods and demons.  Ravana emphasizes Indrajit's resilience, intelligence, and strategic mind, asserting that his son's power rivals his own. He laments the loss of his army, including his kinsmen and generals, emphasizing that his hope now rests solely with Indrajit.  Ravana urges Indrajit to assess Hanuman's strength and his own, advising against conventional warfare and the use of brute force. He suggests a more cunning approach, instructing Indrajit to use his unique abilities and the potent bow to capture Hanuman unharmed.

__data__.text: Indrajit, riding a celestial chariot and armed with a powerful bow, readied himself for battle against Hanuman.  The two formidable warriors, filled with intense rivalry, clashed like legendary adversaries. Hanuman, immense and strong, skillfully evaded Indrajit's arrows, moving with the swiftness of his father, the wind-god.  Indrajit, a skilled archer, unleashed a barrage of sharp, swift arrows. Hanuman, agile and defiant, leaped and dodged, evading the projectiles.  The two warriors, equal in their prowess, engaged in a captivating battle.  Despite Indrajit's relentless attack, Hanuman remained unharmed.  Frustrated by his inability to defeat Hanuman, Indrajit contemplated how to capture the seemingly invincible monkey. He then decided to employ the Brahmastra, a powerful weapon presided over by the creator god Brahma, against Hanuman.

__data__.text: Indrajit, realizing Hanuman's invulnerability, cleverly used the Brahmastra, a divine missile, to bind him.  Hanuman, though unaffected by the missile due to a boon, recognized its power and strategically chose to honor its binding. He reflected on Brahma's favor and accepted his temporary captivity. Confident in his divine protection from Brahma, Indra, and his father, the wind-god, Hanuman saw this capture as an opportunity to meet Ravana.  He allowed himself to be bound by the Rakshasas, feigning distress, furthering his plan to encounter Ravana. The Rakshasas, seeing Hanuman immobile, bound him with ropes.  Hanuman welcomed this, certain that his capture would pique Ravana's curiosity and lead to a meeting.  Ironically, the ropes released Hanuman from the Brahmastra's bind, as the divine weapon could not coexist with another form of restraint.

He lamented that the Rakshasas' ignorance of the missile's workings had ruined his plan.  Hanuman, though free, continued to feign helplessness as the Rakshasas dragged him to Ravana. The Rakshasas, brutal in their handling, struck Hanuman as they brought him before their king. Indrajit presented the seemingly bound Hanuman to Ravana and his court.  The Rakshasas, curious and enraged, questioned Hanuman's identity and purpose, some even suggesting violence.  Hanuman, amidst the chaos, observed Ravana's court, the king's attendants, and the opulent surroundings.  He saw Ravana, radiating power and fury, observing him intently.  Ravana, in turn, saw Hanuman being dragged before him and ordered his advisors to question the captive monkey.

__data__.text: Hanuman, awestruck by Ravana's magnificence, observed the king's opulent attire and powerful presence.  Ravana wore a gold crown studded with pearls, adorned with exquisite jewelry, and dressed in fine silk robes, decorated with red sandalwood paste and intricate designs. His twenty piercing red eyes, sharp teeth, and protruding lips gave him a formidable appearance.  With ten heads, he resembled a majestic mountain, his twenty bejeweled arms like five-headed serpents.  He sat upon a magnificent crystal throne, inlaid with precious stones.  Attendants surrounded him, while his four chief advisors, Durdhara, Prahasta, Mahaparsva, and Nikumbha, sat nearby, offering counsel.  Hanuman noted that despite Ravana's impressive appearance, his unrighteousness ultimately diminished his power.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman observed Ravana, the powerful king of the ogres, seated high upon a throne, like a storm cloud atop a mountain peak. Though surrounded by intimidating ogres, Hanuman was filled with awe.  He marveled at Ravana's shining presence, noting his impressive charm, composure, courage, and splendor.  Hanuman reflected that if Ravana's actions were righteous, he would be a formidable protector, even for the gods themselves. However, Ravana's cruel deeds instilled fear in all beings, and Hanuman recognized his capacity for immense destruction.

__data__.text: Hanuman, observing the full moon ascend to its zenith, saw it cast its brilliant light across the earth.  The moon, resembling a majestic white bull, illuminated the world, dispelling darkness and seemingly minimizing opportunities for wrongdoing.  Its radiant glow, akin to the splendor seen on Mount Mandara, the sea, and the lotus flower, shone brightly, reminiscent of a swan in a silver cage, a lion in its mountain den, or a hero atop an elephant. The fully formed orb, complete with its shadowed hare-like figure, radiated a pure, clear light, having shed the vestiges of frost and cold.  Its brilliance intensified, much like a beast reaching a rocky summit, an elephant entering a forest, or a king returning to his kingdom.  However, even amidst this beautiful moonlit night,  the darkness fostered an increase in the ogres' predatory activities.  As music drifted through the air and virtuous women slept peacefully beside their husbands, the ogres began to stir, their behavior growing increasingly erratic and violent.  Hanuman observed their opulent mansions filled with chariots, horses, and golden seats, witnessing the ogres' boisterous revelry, their boasts, and their aggressive posturing toward one another.  They engaged in physical displays of strength, adorned themselves in various attire, and practiced their archery skills.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman surveyed the opulent city of Lanka. He observed women engaged in various activities: some applied fragrant sandalwood paste, others slept peacefully, some laughed merrily, while others, perhaps angered by lovers' quarrels, seethed.  The city resonated with the trumpeting of majestic elephants and the presence of noble individuals like Vibhishana. Yet, amidst this bustling activity, Hanuman also noted the presence of formidable warriors, their restless energy reminiscent of coiled serpents.  He saw the city's inhabitants, intelligent and eloquent, devoted to their pursuits and adorned in diverse, elegant attire. Hanuman was pleased by the sight of these graceful individuals, many possessing admirable virtues, even though some bore physical imperfections.  He then observed the women of Lanka, elegantly dressed and adorned, their hearts pure and dignified, their affections divided between their beloved and fine drinks. Some women, radiant with beauty and virtue, embraced their husbands in the intimacy of night. Others, held in high esteem by their husbands, sat comfortably, their devotion evident. Hanuman noted the varied appearances of the women, some clad in shimmering gold, others pale with longing for absent husbands. He saw joyous women, adorned with flowers, reunited with their beloved. Their faces shone like the moon, their eyes sparkled, and their ornaments glittered.  Despite this panorama of life in Lanka, Hanuman could not find Sita, the noble princess descended from a virtuous lineage, her beauty akin to a blossoming flower.  His search continued.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman, despite seeing many within Lanka, failed to find Sita, the devoted wife of Rama.  Sita, steadfast in her love for Rama, remained ever present in his thoughts.  Now, suffering in captivity, her once adorned throat was choked with tears of sorrow. Her former radiance, once comparable to a dancing peahen, was now dimmed, obscured like a crescent moon behind clouds or a gold streak dulled by dust.  The pain of her absence, a wound upon Rama's heart, was mirrored in her own suffering, visible like a scar. Even as she was carried to Lanka, her inherent nobility shone through, like a streak of cloud against the wind.  Hanuman, not finding her, was overcome with sorrow.

__data__.text: Ravana, enraged by Hanuman's presence and destruction, questioned his identity and motives.  Unsure of Hanuman's true nature, Ravana wondered if he was a divine being like Nandi or a powerful demon in disguise.  He commanded Prahasta to question Hanuman. Prahasta, attempting to reassure Hanuman, inquired about his origins and purpose, suggesting he could be a messenger from Indra, Kubera, Yama, Varuna, or even Vishnu, observing that Hanuman's power seemed to exceed that of a typical monkey.  Prahasta promised Hanuman freedom if he revealed the truth, but warned of dire consequences if he lied.

He stated he was brought before Ravana as a captive despite being freed from the missile's effect. Finally, Hanuman identified himself as Sri Rama's messenger, urging Ravana to listen to his message.

Canto LI
Narrating the story of Sri Rama from the latter’s entry into the forest, to his
own sight of Sita being borne away by Ravana through the air over the
Rsyamika mountain, and celebrating the glory of Sri Rama, Hanuman
points out to Ravana that if he longed to survive he should
restore Sita to Sri Rama and that he should be prepared for the

__data__.text: Hanuman, possessing great strength, calmly addressed the powerful, ten-headed Ravana. He conveyed a message from Sugriva, the monkey king, inquiring about Ravana's well-being. Hanuman then relayed Sugriva's message, advising Ravana on a course of action beneficial for both his present and future life.  He recounted the story of King Dasaratha, a benevolent ruler, whose eldest son, Rama, was exiled to the Dandaka Forest with his wife Sita and brother Lakshmana.  Sita, daughter of King Janaka, was lost in the Janasthana forest. While searching for her, Rama and Lakshmana met Sugriva on Mount Rsyamuka.  Sugriva promised to help Rama find Sita, and in return, Rama pledged to restore Sugriva's kingship. Rama fulfilled his promise by slaying Vali and reinstating Sugriva as king of the monkeys and bears.  Hanuman reminded Ravana of his prior acquaintance with Vali, the foremost of monkeys, whom Rama killed with a single arrow. Now, Sugriva, eager to locate Sita and honor his promise, has sent search parties in all directions.

__data__.text: Hanuman continues his message to Ravana, emphasizing the vast scale of the search for Sita.  Countless monkeys are scouring the earth, sky, and even the underworld. These monkey warriors possess incredible speed and strength, some rivaling Garuda and the wind itself.  Hanuman identifies himself as the son of the wind-god and reveals he crossed the vast ocean to Lanka, driven by his determination to find Sita. He states that he has indeed located Sita in Ravana's garden.  Hanuman urges Ravana to consider the consequences of his actions. He appeals to Ravana's wisdom, advising him against keeping another man's wife, reminding him of the principles of righteousness. He cautions Ravana against actions that are harmful and destructive.  Hanuman warns Ravana of the power of Rama and Lakshmana, questioning who could withstand their wrath. He asserts that no one can offend Rama and expect to remain unscathed.  Hanuman implores Ravana to return Sita to Rama, presenting this as a course of action that would benefit him in the long run.  He reiterates that he has found the grieving Sita, comparing her to a dangerous serpent within Ravana's home, warning that she is too powerful for Ravana to control, just as poisoned food cannot be digested.

__data__.text: Hanuman continues his warning to Ravana, stressing the gravity of stealing Sita.  He reminds Ravana of the spiritual merits and long life earned through austerities, which are now at risk.  Ravana's immunity to death, even from gods and demons, stems from these austerities, yet he jeopardizes it all.  Hanuman highlights the difference in nature between Rama and Sugriva, who are not divine beings but human and monkey respectively, questioning how Ravana can possibly hope to survive against them.  He points out that good deeds cannot undo the consequences of unrighteous acts, and that Ravana will inevitably face the repercussions of his actions. Ravana has already enjoyed the benefits of his past virtues, but now the negative consequences of his current misdeed, the abduction of Sita, are imminent. Hanuman urges Ravana to remember the defeats of the Janasthana ogres and Vali, and to consider the alliance between Rama and Sugriva as a sign of the forces aligned against him.  Hanuman asserts his own power, claiming he could single-handedly destroy Lanka, but clarifies that Rama's intention is not mere destruction.  He reveals Rama's vow to annihilate those responsible for harming Sita, emphasizing that even Indra could not escape the consequences of wronging Rama.  Hanuman then likens Sita to Kalaratri, the goddess of destruction, warning that she represents doom for Lanka.  He describes Sita as a noose of death around Ravana's neck and advises him to prioritize his own safety by returning her.  Hanuman concludes with a vivid image of Lanka, already consumed by Sita's moral power and ignited by Rama's anger.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman implores Ravana to reconsider his actions, urging him not to destroy his kingdom, family, and possessions for the sake of keeping Sita.  He emphasizes the truthfulness of his words, particularly as a messenger of Rama. Hanuman then declares Rama's immense power, capable of destroying and recreating the entire universe. He asserts that no being, be it god, demon, or any other creature, can withstand Rama's might in battle.  He reminds Ravana of Rama's divine status and the futility of opposing him, highlighting that even powerful deities like Brahma, Rudra, and Indra would be unable to stand against him.  Finally, the verses describe Ravana's furious reaction to Hanuman's bold words, ordering the monkey's death.

Canto LII
Provoked by the harsh words of Hanuman, Ravana orders him to be put
to death. Vibhisana, however, exhorts the latter to desist from
this dastardly act, pointing out that the killing of an
envoy is forbidden by the Sastras

__data__.text: Ravana, enraged by Hanuman's defiant words, ordered his execution. Vibhisana, however, disapproved of this command and contemplated the impending, unjust act. Recognizing the gravity of the situation, he carefully considered his duty.  Vibhisana, eloquent and wise, then implored his elder brother Ravana to reconsider. He argued against harming Hanuman, emphasizing that executing a messenger is a transgression against dharma (righteousness) and against the conduct of noble rulers. He reminded Ravana of his wisdom and knowledge of royal duties, urging him to distinguish between right and wrong. Vibhisana pointed out the futility of scriptural knowledge if overcome by anger and advised Ravana to carefully determine a just punishment, rather than resorting to immediate execution.  However, Ravana, furious upon hearing Vibhisana's plea, retorted that killing the wicked is not a sin and declared his intention to slay Hanuman. Vibhisana, recognizing the flaws in Ravana's reasoning, prepared to offer a further, wiser response.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:

Canto LII
Provoked by the harsh words of Hanuman, Ravana orders him to be put

this dastardly act, pointing out that the killing of an
envoy is forbidden by the Sastras

__data__.text: Vibhisana continued his impassioned plea to Ravana, emphasizing that messengers should never be harmed, regardless of their message. He argued that numerous alternative punishments existed, such as mutilation, flogging, shaving the head, or branding, but death was never an option.  He appealed to Ravana's wisdom and knowledge of dharma (righteousness), questioning how someone so learned could be swayed by anger. Vibhisana lauded Ravana's knowledge of scripture and his prowess, reminding him of his victories against gods and demons. He asserted that no one dared defy Ravana, highlighting the monkey's insignificance and suggesting that punishment should be directed at those who sent him, not the messenger himself. Vibhisana further argued that killing Hanuman would prevent any further communication, effectively cutting off a valuable source of information.  He concluded by suggesting that instead of focusing on Hanuman, Ravana should direct his attention towards the gods, especially Indra.  Finally, Vibhisana pointed out that Hanuman's death would eliminate the catalyst for war with Rama and Lakshmana, implying it would be strategically unwise.

__data__.text: Vibhisana continues to advise Ravana against killing Hanuman. He argues that it is unbecoming of Ravana, whose power surpasses even the gods and demons, to engage in such a trivial act of violence against the monkey envoy. Instead, he suggests that Ravana should dispatch some of his best warriors to capture Rama and Lakshmana, showcasing his dominance to his enemies. Ravana, surprisingly, takes his brother's counsel to heart.

__data__.text: Hanuman, captured by Ravana's forces, is punished by having his tail set on fire.  Vibhishana advises against killing Hanuman, suggesting a less severe punishment.  Ravana orders Hanuman's tail to be wrapped in rags, soaked in oil, and lit. As the ogres carry out the order, Hanuman grows in size.  He then uses his burning tail as a weapon, striking the ogres. Despite being bound, Hanuman considers breaking free and fighting back, but also reflects on his duty to his master, Rama.  He reasons that if his capture serves Rama's purpose, the ogres will not face retribution for their actions.

__data__.text: Hanuman, resolved to complete his reconnaissance of Lanka, willingly allows the Rakshasas to bind and parade him through the city.  His captors, making a spectacle of him, announce his supposed crimes with conches and drums as they drag him through streets and squares.  The curious citizens of Lanka – men, women, and children – flock to see the captured monkey.  As Hanuman’s tail is set alight, the news reaches Sita, relayed by Rakshasa women. Distressed by the news of Hanuman’s plight, Sita prays to Agni, the god of fire, invoking her devotion to Rama and her past austerities, to protect Hanuman from the flames.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Sita, anguished by Hanuman's predicament, fervently prayed to Agni, the fire god, for his protection. She appealed to Agni's compassion for her, invoking her virtuous conduct and unwavering devotion to Rama. She hoped that her past austerities and her husband's inherent righteousness would move Agni to spare Hanuman.  Miraculously, the flames began to burn gently, their heat replaced by a comforting coolness.  Even the wind, Hanuman’s divine parent, blew cold, soothing Sita’s worries.  Hanuman himself marveled at the fire's inability to harm him. He pondered this strange phenomenon, observing the intense flames that brought no pain, only a sensation akin to ice.  He reasoned that this must be due to Rama's divine influence, the same power that had manifested the miraculous mountain during his ocean crossing. Recalling the respect shown to Rama by both the ocean and Mount Mainaka, Hanuman concluded that Agni, too, must be demonstrating reverence by its gentle touch. He also attributed his safety to Sita's compassion, Rama's inherent power, and his father Vayu's friendship with Agni.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman, briefly pondering his capture, questioned how such a powerful being like himself could be bound by the ogres. Determined to retaliate, he broke free from his bonds, leaping into the air with a mighty roar. He reached the towering city gate, now deserted by the ogre guards. Shrinking his form to a minuscule size, he slipped free of his restraints and then returned to his colossal, mountain-like stature.  Spotting an iron-studded club at the gate, he wielded it to vanquish the remaining guards.  Surrounded by the fiery glow emanating from his burning tail, Hanuman, the fierce warrior, surveyed Lanka, radiating a brilliance akin to the sun.

Making up his mind to set fire to the city of Lanka, which was the only
work left for him to do, and coming out into the open, Hanuman
burns the entire city barring the abode of Vibhisana.
Astonished to see the city burning, the gods and
Gandharvas (celestial musicians) are
filled with supreme joy

Canto LIV
Making up his mind to set fire to the city of Lanka, which was the only
work left for him to do, and coming out into the open, Hanuman
burns the entire city barring the abode of Vibhisana.
Astonished to see the city burning, the gods and

filled with supreme joy

__data__.text: Hanuman, having successfully found Sita, considered his next move.  He wondered what else he could do to further weaken his enemies, the Rakshasas.  He had already damaged the Ashok Vatika grove, killed several Rakshasas, and diminished their army. Destroying Lanka's citadel remained his final objective. He reasoned that demolishing the fort would make his earlier efforts, including crossing the ocean and vandalizing the grove, worthwhile. It would also ensure the success of his mission to find Sita. Hanuman then decided to use the fire on his tail, which burned his enemies but didn't harm him, to set Lanka ablaze.  He began systematically setting fire to the buildings, moving quickly from rooftop to rooftop.  Undeterred, he surveyed the mansions and gardens as he spread the flames.  With great force, he set fire to the homes of prominent Rakshasas, including Prahasta, Mahaparswa, Vajradamshtra, Suka, Sarana, Indrajit, Jambumali, and Sumali.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman continued his fiery rampage through Lanka, specifically targeting the homes of prominent Rakshasa figures.  He set ablaze the residences of Rasmiketu, SuryaSatru, Hraswakarna, Damstra, Romasa, Yuddhonmatta, Matta, Dhwajagriva, Vidyujjihva, Ghora, Hastimukha, Karala, Visala, Sonitaksa, Kumbhakarana, Makaraksa, Narantaka, Kumbha, Nikumbha, Yajnasatru, and Brahmasatru. Notably, he spared the house of Vibhishana.  Hanuman then proceeded to burn the opulent mansions of wealthy Rakshasas, consuming their riches. Finally, he reached the magnificent palace of Ravana, which was adorned with jewels and resembled the majestic mountains Meru and Mandara.  There, Hanuman unleashed the fire from his tail, and with a roar like a doomsday cloud, the flames spread rapidly, fueled by the wind, engulfing Ravana's palace in a destructive inferno.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman, empowered by the wind, spread the fire throughout Lanka.  The grand mansions, adorned with gold, gems, and pearls, crumbled and fell like the celestial homes of Siddhas.  Panic-stricken Rakshasas ran to save their homes, their wealth and pride lost.  They cried out that the fire god himself had come in the form of a monkey.  Women and children fell from the burning buildings.  Some women, engulfed in flames, appeared like lightning against the smoke.  Hanuman watched as molten gold, silver, diamonds, corals, and cat’s-eye gems flowed from the burning structures. He felt no fatigue, just as fire is never sated by consuming wood and grass.  Similarly, the earth received the bodies of the slain Rakshasas. The inferno, sparked by Hanuman, raged like the fire that destroyed the mythical city of Tripura.  It climbed Mount Trikuta, spreading its flames like a doomsday fire, fueled by the bodies of the Rakshasas, and reaching towards the heavens. The flames, bright as a million suns, filled the air with thunderous roars.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: The fire, intensely bright and scarlet, spread rapidly towards the sky, creating massive columns of dark smoke that resembled blue lotuses. The Rakshasas, witnessing this terrifying spectacle, believed Hanuman to be a god – Indra, Yama, Varuna, the fire god within Shiva’s third eye, Surya, Kubera, Chandra, or even Kala, the Time-Spirit himself. Some wondered if it was Brahma, disguised as a monkey, unleashing his wrath upon them. Others speculated if it was Vishnu's divine energy, taking the form of a monkey to destroy the Rakshasas.  The Rakshasas gathered before Ravana, lamenting the destruction of their city, their homes, and the loss of life. Lanka, once vibrant, was now consumed by flames, its warriors defeated and scattered. Hanuman, having set the city ablaze, stood amidst the devastation, witnessing the scene of destruction and chaos.  He had destroyed the Asoka grove, defeated powerful Rakshasas in battle, and set fire to the magnificent buildings of Lanka. Having completed his task, he turned his thoughts towards Rama.  The gods, witnessing Hanuman's extraordinary feat of strength and speed, rejoiced and praised the valiant son of the wind god.

__data__.text: Hanuman, having completed his mission of setting Lanka ablaze, was a sight to behold. The gods, celestial musicians, and other divine beings rejoiced at this extraordinary feat.  With immense power and radiance, Hanuman stood atop a building, flames emanating from his tail, resembling the sun with its aura. After inflicting significant damage upon Lanka, he extinguished his fiery tail in the ocean. The gods, along with celestial sages and seers, marveled at the sight of Lanka reduced to ashes, some even mistaking Hanuman for the fire of cosmic destruction.

__data__.text: Hanuman, observing the burning city of Lanka and its panicked inhabitants, was overcome with worry and self-reproach. He lamented his impulsive act of setting the city ablaze, recognizing the destructive nature of unchecked anger.  He reflected on the virtue of controlling one's temper, comparing it to extinguishing a fire with water.  Hanuman condemned his anger, acknowledging that it can lead even the wisest to commit terrible deeds, from harming elders to insulting the pious.  He further berated himself for his lack of foresight, fearing that his actions had inadvertently caused Sita's death and undermined Rama's mission.  Consumed by guilt, Hanuman believed he had destroyed the very purpose of his journey and considered ending his own life.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Overwhelmed by despair, Hanuman considers ending his life by fire, water, or at the hands of sea creatures. He believes he cannot face Sugriva, Rama, or Lakshmana, having ruined their mission through his rash act of burning Lanka. Hanuman laments his impulsive anger, a characteristic of his simian nature, known throughout the three worlds. He blames his uncontrolled rage for Sita's presumed demise, even though he was capable of protecting her. Hanuman predicts a cascade of tragic consequences:  Rama and Lakshmana perishing due to Sita’s death, followed by Sugriva and his kin, and then the virtuous Bharata and Shatrughna succumbing to grief. Ultimately, he foresees the destruction of the righteous Ikshvaku dynasty and widespread suffering throughout the world. He believes he has lost all merit and become an instrument of global destruction. However, amidst his despair, Hanuman recalls positive omens and considers that Sita, protected by her own virtue and Rama's power, may have survived the fire, just as he himself did. He reasons that fire cannot harm fire, and Sita's purity and Rama's strength would shield her from the flames.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman, reflecting on Sita's virtue, questioned how the fire could harm someone so revered by Rama and his brothers. He recalled how the fire hadn't even burned his own tail and wondered if Sita's purity might have protected her.  Remembering the miraculous appearance of Mainaka mountain amidst the sea, Hanuman reasoned that Sita's devotion and truthful nature would shield her from the flames. He overheard celestial bards marveling at the extent of the fire's destruction across Lanka, yet confirming that Sita remained unharmed.  This news filled Hanuman with immense joy and relief, reaffirming his earlier hopes based on positive omens and Sita's inherent strength.  Knowing Sita was safe, Hanuman, his mission successful, decided to return to Rama.

takes a leap across the sea

Canto LVI

Hanuman sees Sita once more and bidding adieu to her,
takes a leap across the sea

__data__.text: Hanuman reassured Sita of her safety. As he prepared to leave, Sita, deeply concerned, urged him to stay, believing his presence lessened her immeasurable grief. She worried about his safe return and the immense challenge of crossing the ocean, doubting even Sugriva and his army's ability to achieve this feat.  She acknowledged Hanuman, Garuda, and the wind-god as the only beings capable of such a crossing.  Sita questioned Hanuman, an expert in action, about his plan to overcome this seemingly insurmountable obstacle. While acknowledging Hanuman's individual capability to rescue her, Sita expressed her desire for Rama to achieve this victory, leading his forces to conquer Lanka and reclaim her, an act she deemed truly worthy of him.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:

Canto LVI

Hanuman sees Sita once more and bidding adieu to her,

__data__.text: Hanuman advised Sita to remain courageous so that Rama, known for his valor, could showcase his true prowess.  He then reassured Sita that Sugriva, the leader of the monkeys and bears, was committed to her rescue.  A vast army was being assembled and would arrive soon, led by Sugriva.  Furthermore, Rama and Lakshmana would join the battle and destroy Lanka with their arrows.  Hanuman confidently predicted Rama's victory over Ravana and his forces, promising Sita's safe return to Ayodhya. He urged her to be patient and have faith, assuring her that she would witness Rama's triumph over Ravana.  He compared their reunion to that of Rohini and the moon-god, emphasizing its certainty.  Finally, Hanuman reiterated Rama's imminent arrival with his monkey and bear allies, promising the end of her sorrow.  Having thus comforted Sita, Hanuman prepared to depart.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:

Canto LVI

Hanuman sees Sita once more and bidding adieu to her,
takes a leap across the sea

__data__.text: Hanuman, having demonstrated his immense strength by defeating powerful demons and making his presence felt throughout Lanka, instilled confidence in Sita.  He then prepared to return across the ocean.  Driven by a powerful desire to rejoin Rama, Hanuman, the mightiest of monkeys, ascended Mount Arista.  The mountain's description emphasizes its grandeur and natural beauty:  its peaks shrouded in clouds, adorned with diverse trees and vibrant blossoms, echoing with the sounds of waterfalls and teeming with wildlife.  As Hanuman climbed, eager to return with news of Sita, the mountain groaned under his immense weight, its rocks crumbling beneath his feet.  He then surveyed the vast ocean, preparing for his tremendous leap.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto LVI

Hanuman sees Sita once more and bidding adieu to her,
takes a leap across the sea

Canto LVI

Hanuman sees Sita once more and bidding adieu to her,
takes a leap across the sea

__data__.text: As Hanuman prepared for his leap, his immense power caused widespread disruption on Mount Arista. The trees, laden with blossoms, were uprooted and thrown to the ground as if struck by lightning.  Lions roared, their cries echoing through the sky as the mountain pressed in upon their caves.  The Vidyadhara women, their clothing disheveled and ornaments scattered, fled in terror.  Large, venomous snakes with fiery tongues were crushed beneath the pressure.  The Kinnaras, Nagas, Gandharvas, Yaksas, and Vidyadharas abandoned the mountain, taking refuge in the sky as the peak itself began to sink under Hanuman's weight.  The once towering mountain, ten yojanas wide and thirty yojanas high, became level with the earth.  With the ocean before him, its shores lashed by waves, Hanuman launched himself into the air, ready to cross the vast expanse of water.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman, powerful and swift, crossed the sky like a winged mountain, touching Mainaka mountain on his way.  He roared with triumphant joy upon seeing Mahendra mountain, signaling his successful return to the waiting monkeys. Jambavan reassured the startled monkeys that Hanuman's roar indicated his mission's success.  Landing on Mahendra mountain, Hanuman greeted Jambavan and the others and briefly recounted his discovery of Sita.  Eager to hear the full story, Prince Angada and the other monkeys settled down on a large flat rock. Continuing his journey, Hanuman soared through the sky, which seemed like a vast ocean, with celestial bodies resembling aquatic features: constellations like water lilies and swans, the moon a white lily, the sun a waterfowl, and clouds like duckweed. He moved with immense speed, seemingly consuming the sky itself, brushing against the moon, and parting the clouds.  The sky displayed clouds of various hues - white, pink, blue, reddish-brown, green, and dark. Hanuman, frequently disappearing and reappearing within the clouds, resembled the waxing and waning moon, his white clothing adding to the illusion. He continued his flight, tearing through the cloud formations like a powerful bird.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman, having vanquished powerful demons and made his name known throughout Lanka, caused chaos and grief for Ravana. After meeting Sita and defeating Ravana's strongest warriors, Hanuman, full of energy, crossed the sea again, touching Mainaka mountain on his way.  Like an arrow shot from a bow, he approached Mahendra mountain.  He roared like thunder, filling the sky with the sound.  Reaching the area where his fellow monkeys awaited, Hanuman, eager to see them, let out a great cry and waved his tail, his roar shaking the sky and sun.  The monkeys on the northern shore, waiting anxiously for Hanuman’s return, heard the whooshing sound of his powerful flight, like a massive wind-driven cloud.  Hearing Hanuman’s thunderous roar, the monkeys, distressed with worry, became filled with anticipation and joy at his return. Jambavan, overjoyed, then addressed the waiting monkeys.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman's triumphant return was evident in his powerful roar, causing the waiting monkeys to leap with joy and anticipation.  They scrambled from tree to tree, eager to greet their hero.  Standing on branches, they waved their garments in celebration. Hanuman, with a roar like wind escaping a mountain cavern, descended from the sky like a storm cloud towards Mahendra mountain. The monkeys, awestruck, watched him land with the grace of a mountain settling upon the earth, near a refreshing spring.  Overjoyed, they surrounded Hanuman, their faces beaming with happiness. They brought him gifts of fruits and roots, while some roared with delight and others brought branches for him to rest upon.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman, the mighty monkey, respectfully greeted the elders, including Jambavan and Prince Angada.  Honored by them and the other monkeys, Hanuman shared the news that he had found Sita. He sat down with Angada and described Sita's condition: she was residing in the Asoka grove, guarded by fearsome ogresses, longing for Rama.  Wearing a single braid, she was weakened by fasting, her hair matted and dusty, her body thin.  This news brought immense joy to the monkeys.  Some roared, some cheered, some bellowed.  They waved their tails, embraced Hanuman, and celebrated his return. Angada praised Hanuman's unmatched courage and valor in crossing the vast sea and returning safely, calling him their savior.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman's successful mission brought immense relief and joy to the monkey army.  Jambavan, Angada, Hanuman, and the other monkeys gathered on the mountain rocks.  Eager to hear the details of Hanuman's journey across the ocean and his encounter with Sita and Ravana in Lanka, the monkeys respectfully gathered around Angada and Hanuman, their faces turned towards Hanuman in anticipation. Angada, adorned with armlets, sat among the throng like Indra among the gods.  The scene was filled with joy and excitement, the mountain peak seeming to glow with the reflected happiness of the assembled monkeys.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

He inquires about Sita's well-being, Ravana's treatment of her, how Hanuman located her, and her response. Jambavan also seeks Hanuman's advice on what to report to Rama and Lakshmana upon their arrival in Lanka.  Prompted by Jambavan, Hanuman begins his narration, describing his leap across the ocean from Mount Mahendra.

__data__.text: During his flight to Lanka, Hanuman encountered a golden mountain, Mainaka, obstructing his path.  He initially prepared to destroy the mountain but Mainaka addressed him, revealing himself as a friend of Hanuman's father, the wind-god. Mainaka explained that he and other mountains once had wings and roamed freely, causing disruption.  Indra, however, clipped their wings. Mainaka was saved by Hanuman's father.  He urged Hanuman to assist Rama, whom he described as a righteous and powerful warrior. After this exchange, Hanuman continued his journey, and Mainaka, both in his human and mountain form, disappeared.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman, having left Mainaka, continued his journey to Lanka at great speed.  He then encountered the serpentine goddess Surasa in the middle of the ocean. She declared that he was destined to be her food, a claim supported by the gods.  Hanuman, appearing respectful and slightly afraid, explained his mission to find Sita, wife of Rama, who had been kidnapped by Ravana. He promised to return to Surasa after completing his task for Rama.  Surasa, however, challenged him, stating no one escapes her.  Hanuman, initially ten yojanas (eighty miles) wide, increased his size by half.  Surasa widened her mouth even further to consume him.  In response, Hanuman shrunk himself to the size of a thumb, entered her mouth, and promptly exited.  Surasa, impressed by his cleverness, then reverted to her true form and addressed Hanuman.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Surasa, a fearsome ogress, challenged Hanuman, demanding he become her meal.  Hanuman, clever and respectful, agreed but then grew larger than her gaping maw.  As Surasa expanded her mouth further, Hanuman shrunk to the size of a thumb, zipped through her mouth, and then escaped into the sky. Defeated, Surasa acknowledged Hanuman's wit and blessed his journey to find Sita.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman, having defeated Surasa, heard voices from the sky celebrating his victory over the demoness Simhika.  Remembering his mission to find Sita, he continued his journey and reached the shores of Lanka.  Unseen by the fearsome ogre guards, Hanuman entered the city at sunset. A powerful, dark-skinned female entity confronted him.  Hanuman quickly defeated her.  She then revealed herself as the spirit of Lanka and prophesied Hanuman's victory over all the ogres. Despite searching throughout the night, even within Ravana's palace, Hanuman couldn't find Sita. Dejected, he then spotted a walled garden filled with Ashoka trees.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

daughter and returned to Mount Mahendra

__data__.text: Hanuman, climbing a tree, saw a grove of plantain trees and then spotted Sita.  She appeared thin and pale from fasting, wearing a single garment, her hair dusty.  Grief and worry were evident.  She was surrounded by grotesque, cruel ogresses, like a deer cornered by tigers.  Sita, with her doe-like eyes, wore a single braid, lay on the bare ground, and looked as fragile as a lotus plant in winter.  Rejected by Ravana and despairing, she seemed resigned to death. Hanuman watched her from his perch in the tree.  Suddenly, he heard loud voices, the clinking of jewelry, and other commotion coming from Ravana's quarters. Alarmed, Hanuman shrank his size and hid amongst the thick leaves.  Ravana then arrived with his wives at the place where Sita was held captive.  Upon seeing the ogre king, Sita instinctively drew her knees up and covered herself with her arms.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Ravana, approaching the captive Sita, prostrated himself and implored her to favor him.  Sita, terrified and desperate, found no solace in his presence.  Enraged by his advances, she retorted that he dared address her, the wife of the mighty Rama and daughter-in-law of King Dasaratha, with such a disrespectful proposal. She questioned his courage, highlighting his cowardly act of abducting her in Rama's absence.  She declared Rama's superiority, stating that Ravana wasn't even fit to be his servant.  Infuriated by her defiance, Ravana, eyes blazing with anger, raised his fist to strike her.  The women attendants cried out in alarm.  Mandodari, Ravana's wife, intervened, restraining him and attempting to pacify his anger.  She urged him to enjoy her company instead of pursuing Sita, suggesting that Sita was no better than herself or any of the other celestial women available to him.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: After Ravana's forceful exit, the grotesque ogresses guarding Sita threatened her with harsh words.  Sita, however, dismissed their intimidation, their shouts and threats meaningless to her. The ogresses, frustrated by Sita's unwavering resolve, reported her death wish to Ravana.  Exhausted and defeated, the guards fell asleep. While they slept, Sita, deeply grieved and loyal to Rama, lamented her plight.  Among the sleeping ogresses, Trijata awoke and urged the others not to harm Sita. Trijata recounted a terrifying dream that foretold the ogres' destruction and Rama's victory, suggesting that Sita alone could protect them from Rama's wrath.  Trijata proposed that they seek Sita's favor, believing that such a dream indicated Sita's imminent release from suffering and that showing respect to her would be beneficial.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman, encouraged by Trijata's dream foretelling Rama's victory and the ogresses' reliance on Sita for protection, felt renewed hope.  He devised a plan to speak with Sita. He began by praising Rama's lineage, prompting a tearful Sita to question his identity and purpose. Hanuman revealed himself as Sugriva's emissary, sent by Rama with a ring as a token.  Upon recognizing the ring, Sita expressed her desire to be rescued by Rama. Hanuman then respectfully requested a token from Sita to bring back to Rama, and she offered him a jewel.

__data__.text: Sita gave Hanuman a jewel and tearfully shared details known only to Rama. Hanuman bowed to Sita and circumambulated her, intending to return. Sita urged him to quickly relay her plight to Rama and Lakshmana, emphasizing her dwindling hope and limited time.  Angered by Sita's desperation, Hanuman grew to an immense size and began destroying Ravana's pleasure garden.  The ogresses, witnessing the devastation and Hanuman's power, alerted Ravana, urging him to swiftly punish the monkey before he could escape.  Ravana, in turn, dispatched his formidable Kinkara warriors.

__data__.text: Hanuman recounted his escalating conflict with Ravana's forces. He single-handedly defeated eighty thousand ogres armed with various weapons. The surviving few reported the devastating loss to Ravana.  Enraged, Hanuman then destroyed a sacred sanctuary, killing one hundred more ogres.  Ravana responded by sending Jambumali and his followers, but Hanuman swiftly defeated them as well.  Next, Ravana deployed his minister's sons and an infantry regiment, who also met their demise at Hanuman's hands.  Finally, Ravana sent his mighty son Aksa and other ogres into battle.  Hanuman overpowered Aksa, seizing him by the feet, whirling him around, and then crushing him.

__data__.text: Hanuman, captured after his destructive rampage, was brought before Ravana. Indrajit, Ravana's son, had subdued Hanuman using Brahma's weapon after witnessing Hanuman's overwhelming power.  Hanuman then explained to Ravana he was a messenger from Rama and Sugriva, seeking Sita and offering counsel from Sugriva in the spirit of righteousness and mutual benefit. He further detailed his alliance with Rama while residing on Mount Rsyamuka.

__data__.text: Hanuman recounts to Jambavan and the assembled monkeys how he conveyed his message to Ravana.  He reminded Ravana that Sita had been kidnapped and that he should return her. He explained Rama's alliance with Sugriva, forged after Rama aided Sugriva in reclaiming his kingdom from Vali.  Hanuman stressed that he was acting as Sugriva's envoy, offering Ravana a chance to return Sita peacefully before facing the might of the monkey army.  Ravana, enraged by Hanuman's words, ordered his execution. However, Vibhisana, Ravana's wise brother, intervened, reminding Ravana that killing a messenger, especially one who had committed no offense, violated all codes of conduct.  He argued that even for a serious transgression, the prescribed punishment for a messenger was mutilation, not death.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Ravana, advised by Vibhisana against killing Hanuman, instead ordered his tail to be set on fire.  Hanuman's tail was wrapped in cloth and set alight.  Despite being bound and struck by the Rakshasas, Hanuman felt no pain, focused as he was on observing the city.  He was paraded through Lanka's streets with his burning tail. Hanuman then shrunk his size, broke free, and grabbed an iron bar, attacking the Rakshasas.  Using his flaming tail, he set fire to the city.  Believing Sita to have perished in the flames, Hanuman despaired, thinking he had ruined Rama's mission. He then overheard celestial bards proclaiming that Sita was still alive, sparking a renewed sense of hope and prompting him to reconsider the situation.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman recounts how several auspicious signs convinced him that Sita remained unharmed despite the fire engulfing Lanka. He felt no pain from his burning tail, his heart was filled with joy, and sweet fragrances wafted through the air.  He also recalled past experiences where such omens proved true. Coupled with Sita's known devotion to Rama and the pronouncements of all-seeing celestial beings, Hanuman was certain of Sita's survival.  He confirms having seen Sita again and receiving her permission to depart. After reaching Mount Arista in Lanka, Hanuman began his journey back, eager to rejoin his companions.  He traveled the celestial path used by the wind, sun, moon, and other divine beings before finally finding them. He attributes his success to Rama's grace, the monkey's strength, and Sugriva's guidance.  Hanuman concludes his narrative, urging the others to complete any remaining tasks.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman, addressing the assembled monkeys, recounts Sita's unwavering virtue and resilience, emphasizing her ability to endure hardship and her potential for righteous anger. He notes that even Ravana, powerful as he is, could not diminish her through his touch.  Hanuman declares his confidence in his ability to single-handedly defeat Ravana and destroy Lanka, but emphasizes the greater potential for victory with the combined strength of the monkey army. He expresses his determination to vanquish Ravana, his army, his sons, and his brothers in battle, even countering Indrajit's formidable arsenal of divine missiles.  He urges the monkeys to join him in reclaiming Sita and returning her to Rama and Lakshmana.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman continues to rally the monkey army, boasting of their collective strength and his own prowess. He asserts that his power, if unleashed with their approval, could devastate even the gods, let alone Ravana's demonic forces.  He emphasizes his restraint, awaiting their consent before unleashing his full potential.  Hanuman then extols the unwavering strength of Jambavan, declaring that even the mightiest forces of nature could not shake him in battle. He further praises Angada, Nila, Mainda, and Dwivida, highlighting their individual capabilities and asserting their unmatched power against any opponent.  Recounting his exploits in Lanka, Hanuman describes how he single-handedly ravaged the city and boldly proclaimed Rama's name throughout its streets, declaring his own servitude to the Kosalan king.  Finally, he paints a poignant picture of Sita's plight, emphasizing her sorrow, fragility, and confinement within Ravana's Asoka grove.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman continues his impassioned plea to the monkey army, describing Sita's dire circumstances in Lanka. He portrays her as a virtuous and loyal wife, imprisoned against her will and suffering greatly from separation from Rama.  He emphasizes her physical and emotional distress, highlighting her thinness, pallor, and constant grief. Despite her ordeal, she remains resolute in her devotion to Rama, refusing any interaction with Ravana. Hanuman stresses Sita's unwavering hope and joy upon learning of the alliance between Rama and Sugriva.  He concludes by urging the monkeys to devise a plan to rescue her, suggesting that Ravana's power, though substantial, will ultimately be his undoing due to the sin he committed by abducting Sita.  Rama, Hanuman implies, will be the instrument of Ravana's divinely ordained destruction.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman, possessing immense strength and the ability to change his form, swiftly explored Lanka. He arrived at Ravana's magnificent palace, its walls gleaming like the sun and guarded by fierce sentinels.  The palace was adorned with silver artwork, gold-decorated archways, and beautiful enclosures.  Elephant riders, brave warriors, and resilient horses attended the palace, along with elaborate chariots decorated with animal skins, ivory, gold, silver, and ringing bells.  The palace interior was filled with precious stones, grand seating, chariot sheds, and warrior quarters.  The grounds teemed with various captivating animals and birds.  Disciplined guards and other ogres protected the palace, which was also inhabited by numerous beautiful women.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman surveyed Ravana's opulent palace, bustling with activity and filled with the sounds of jewelry, music, and the sea.  The palace, fragrant with sandalwood and crowded with prominent figures, housed luxurious chambers, precious stones, and chariots.  Hanuman, undeterred, explored the palace grounds, observing the gardens and the residences of Ravana's kin and allies, including Prahasta, Mahaparswa, Kumbhakarna, and Vibhisana, as well as  Mahodara, Virupaksa, Vidyujjihva, Vidyunmali, Vajradamstra, Suka, Sarana, Indrajit, Jambumali, and Sumali.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman continued his exploration of Ravana's palace, leaping from rooftop to rooftop. He surveyed the residences of numerous Rakshasa warriors, including Rasmiketu, Suryasatru, Vajrakaya, Dhumraksa, Sampati, Vidyudripa, Bhima, Ghana, Vighana, Sukanabha, Cakra, Satha, Kapata, Hraswakarna, Damstra, Lomaśa, Yuddhonmatta, Matta, Dhwajagriva, Sadi, Vidyujjihva, Dwijihva, Hastimukha, Karala, and Sonitaksa, noting their wealth and possessions.  After completing this comprehensive survey, Hanuman returned to Ravana's main palace. There, he observed the ogresses guarding Ravana's chambers and the various Rakshasa guards armed with diverse weapons. He also noted the presence of numerous tethered horses, both red and white.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman observed within Ravana's palace highly trained elephants, comparable to Indra's Airavata, their trumpeting echoing like thunder. The palace itself was adorned with gold, shimmering like the morning sun, and filled with palanquins, ornate buildings, pleasure houses, and pens for peacocks.  It was decorated with flagstaffs and overflowing with precious stones and treasures, reminiscent of Kubera's opulent abode.  The palace glittered with the brilliance of gems and Ravana's own splendor. Hanuman also saw golden couches, seats, and vessels, and noted the palace floors were damp with spilled wines and liquors. The air was filled with the sounds of anklets, girdles, clapping, and drums, creating a constant din within the multitude of mansions, which were filled with women and surrounded by spacious enclosures.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Angada, son of Vali, declared that the mighty monkeys Mainda and Dwivida, fueled by a boon of invincibility from Brahma, grew arrogant and defeated the gods.  He asserted that even he alone could destroy Lanka and kill Ravana, and with the other monkeys' help, it would be even easier.  He questioned the point of returning without Sita, reminding everyone of Hanuman's strength and the monkeys' prowess.  He urged them to conquer Lanka, kill Ravana, retrieve Sita, and return victorious. He reasoned that since Hanuman had already vanquished the ogre's forces, there was no reason to delay. They should simply retrieve Sita and return her to Rama and Lakshmana, sparing the other monkeys a difficult journey across the ocean.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Angada declared that the monkey army should proceed to Lanka, defeat Ravana and his forces, and then return to Rama, Lakshmana, and Sugriva with Sita.  Jambavan, however, advised against this course of action. He reminded Angada that their mission was only to search for Sita, not to retrieve her.  He argued that Rama had publicly vowed to personally win back Sita, and taking her back now would invalidate Rama's vow and render Hanuman's efforts meaningless. Jambavan suggested they return to Rama and Sugriva and inform them of Hanuman’s success in locating Sita.  He then gently rebuked Angada, suggesting he align his plan with Rama’s original intent.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

and most beloved of Sugriva, which falls on their way. When the
monkeys began to enjoy the fruits of the grove with the
permission of Prince Angada, Dadhimukha, comes in

__data__.text: The monkey troop, led by Angada and Hanuman, heeded Jambavan's counsel and set off from the Mahendra mountain towards Kiskindha. Filled with joy and purpose, they soared through the air, their immense forms resembling mountains or rutting elephants, their might acknowledged even by celestial beings.  Their mission was clear: to serve Rama and achieve his goals.  They were eager to share the good news of their alliance and prepared for any battle that might come. They soon reached a lush grove, Madhuvana, reminiscent of the celestial Nandana garden. This protected grove, beloved by all, belonged to Sugriva and was guarded by his maternal uncle, the valiant Dadhimukha.  The monkeys were eager to enjoy the grove's bounty and requested Angada's permission to partake of the honey.  After consulting with the elders, including Jambavan, Angada granted them permission.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Allowed to enjoy the fruits of Madhuvana by Angada, the monkeys reveled in the grove's abundance, becoming intoxicated and boisterous.  Their revelry included singing, dancing, laughing, and some even bowing down in reverence, while others fell, ran, leaped, or simply raved. Some monkeys supported each other, while others argued or chased one another through the trees.  The grove became a scene of chaotic merriment, with monkeys swinging through the trees and behaving erratically.  Observing the escalating chaos and destruction of the grove, Dadhimukha, Sugriva's appointed guardian, attempted to intervene.  He scolded the unruly monkeys, attempting to reason with some and physically restraining others.  However, the intoxicated monkeys, unrestrained and disrespectful of Dadhimukha's authority, began to physically assault him, dragging and clawing at him. They continued to consume the grove's resources, leaving it bare.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto LXII
Having entered Madhuvana and partaken of honey, as permitted by Hanuman,
and singing and dancing like drunken people, the monkeys were forbidden
by those guarding the grove and they belaboured the latter. Repulsed by
them, the guards reported the matter to their leader, Dadhimukha,
who appeared on the scene with his entourage and on remonstrating
with the monkeys was thrashed by Angada, who dashed
him to the ground. The guards headed by Dadhimukha
then move en masse to Kiskindha to report the
matter to Sugriva and bow down at the latter’s feet

__data__.text: Hanuman encouraged the monkeys to enjoy the honey in Madhuvana without worry, assuring them of his protection.  Angada supported Hanuman's directive, emphasizing the importance of following the words of one who has successfully completed his mission.  The monkeys enthusiastically agreed, expressing their excitement and approval. They then entered Madhuvana, overpowering the guards.  Driven by Angada's permission and inspired by tales of Sita's presence, the monkeys indulged in the honey and fruits.  Some drank large quantities of honey, others ate the honeycombs, and some discarded them after consuming the honey.  Intoxicated by the honey, some monkeys playfully threw wax at each other, while others clung to tree branches or lay down on leaves, overcome by the effects of the honey.

__data__.text: The monkeys, intoxicated by the honey, engaged in various antics.  Some quarreled and stumbled, while others roared and hooted playfully.  Some lay on the ground, others laughed, and some inexplicably wept.  Their actions were erratic and contradictory.  The guards of Madhuvana, overwhelmed by the monkeys' boisterous behavior, were forced to flee.  They were manhandled and tossed around by the unruly monkeys. The frightened guards reported the incident to their leader, Dadhimukha, describing the monkeys' destructive revelry and their own mistreatment.  Angered by this report, Dadhimukha set out with his entourage to confront the monkeys.  He uprooted a large tree, positioning himself amidst his followers, and advanced towards Madhuvana.  The monkeys, armed with rocks, trees, and stones, furiously charged towards the approaching guards, ready for a confrontation.  Seeing Dadhimukha's anger, Hanuman and the other powerful monkeys rushed forward to meet the challenge.

__data__.text: Angada, enraged, forcefully seized Dadhimukha.  Ignoring familial ties, Angada threw Dadhimukha to the ground and began to roughly drag him.  Badly injured and bleeding, Dadhimukha briefly lost consciousness.  When the monkeys released him, Dadhimukha retreated and instructed his subordinates to return with him to Sugriva and Rama. He intended to report the monkeys' misconduct, confident that Sugriva would punish them for their disrespect of the sacred Madhuvana.  Dadhimukha believed his anger would be satisfied only when the monkeys were punished for defying the royal decree. After issuing these commands, Dadhimukha and his guards immediately departed for Kiskindha, arriving almost instantly at the location of Sugriva, Rama, and Lakshmana. He descended from the sky to address them.

__data__.text: Dadhimukha, the leader of the Madhuvana guards, descended from the sky accompanied by his retinue.  He appeared distressed, his face sorrowful, and his hands clasped above his head in a gesture of supplication.  He immediately prostrated himself before Sugriva, bowing his head to touch Sugriva's feet.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto LXIII
Having heard from the mouth of Dadhimukha the story of Madhuvana
having been laid waste by the monkeys, Sugriva concludes from
the jubilation of the monkeys that Sita has been traced out.
Comforting Dadhimukha, he asks him to send Angada
and others without delay

Canto LXIII
Having heard from the mouth of Dadhimukha the story of Madhuvana
having been laid waste by the monkeys, Sugriva concludes from
the jubilation of the monkeys that Sita has been traced out.
Comforting Dadhimukha, he asks him to send Angada
and others without delay

__data__.text: Sugriva, the leader of the monkeys, was alarmed to see Dadhimukha fall at his feet.  He urged Dadhimukha to rise and reassured him, promising safety and requesting him to speak the truth about what troubled him.  Sugriva inquired about the state of Madhuvana, eager for news.  Reassured by Sugriva's kind words, Dadhimukha explained that the grove, which had never been harmed under the rule of Sugriva's father, Sugriva himself, or even Vali, had been devastated by the monkeys.  He recounted how he and the other guards had tried to intervene, but the monkeys disregarded them, continuing to eat the fruit and honey.  Even when the guards attempted to stop the monkeys’ destruction, they were met with defiance and anger.  Some monkeys not only consumed the honey but also discarded what remained.  The situation escalated, with the guards being assaulted and driven away by the aggressive monkey invaders.  Dadhimukha lamented the beating of the valiant monkey guards and the complete destruction of Madhuvana under Sugriva's rule.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Sugriva deduces that Sita has been found, based on the monkeys' joyous devastation of Madhuvana.  He explains to Lakshmana that the grove's destruction could only mean the monkeys had succeeded in their mission. He points out the guards were overpowered, even Dadhimukha, and reminds Lakshmana that he himself appointed Dadhimukha as guardian.  Sugriva confidently declares Sita has been found by Hanuman, emphasizing Hanuman's unique abilities, intelligence, strength, and knowledge.  He highlights Hanuman's crucial role, along with Angada and Jambavan, in leading the monkey army. He confirms the reports of Madhuvana's destruction by the returning monkeys, led by Angada, after their search of the southern region. Sugriva notes the grove, previously untouchable, was ravaged upon the monkeys' return from Lanka.  He adds that the grove was not merely damaged, but completely consumed, and the guards were overpowered and assaulted.

__data__.text: Sugriva introduces Dadhimukha, the grove's guard, highlighting his eloquence and renowned strength. He tells Lakshmana that Sita must have been found because the monkeys are joyfully feasting in Madhuvana. He reasons that such renowned warriors wouldn't dare touch the sacred grove unless they had succeeded in their mission.  Hearing this news, Rama and Lakshmana are overjoyed.  Sugriva, also delighted, tells Dadhimukha that he's pleased the grove has been enjoyed by the successful monkeys, adding that their actions, though destructive, are understandable given their accomplishment. He instructs Dadhimukha to quickly return and guard the grove, and to immediately send Hanuman and the other monkeys back so he can hear about their success in finding Sita, alongside Rama and Lakshmana. Seeing Rama and Lakshmana's joy, and sensing the imminent success of his plan, Sugriva feels immense happiness.

__data__.text: Dadhimukha, following Sugriva's orders, respectfully greeted Rama, Lakshmana, and Sugriva.  He then swiftly returned to Madhuvana, landing in the forest and finding the previously unruly monkey leaders now subdued and weakened.  Approaching Angada with respect, Dadhimukha urged him not to resent the guards' earlier actions, explaining they acted out of ignorance. He invited Angada and the others to partake of the honey, reminding Angada of his status as crown prince and rightful owner of the grove.

__data__.text: Dadhimukha urged Angada to forgive the guards' prior hostility, attributing it to ignorance. He emphasized Sugriva's current authority, comparing it to Vali's former rule, and reminded Angada of his own high status. Dadhimukha relayed Sugriva's joyful reaction to the news of Angada's return and his command to send everyone to him. Hearing this, Angada addressed the monkeys, acknowledging that Rama likely knew of their arrival.  He deduced this from Dadhimukha's cheerful demeanor and declared they should not delay their return now that their task was complete. He urged the monkeys to return to Kiskindha since they had enjoyed the honey and fulfilled their purpose.  Angada humbly stated his reliance on the monkeys' collective decision regarding his actions, despite his position as crown prince. He added he couldn't order them, as they'd done their duty, and it would be wrong to exert his authority.  The monkeys, pleased with Angada's words, responded even as he spoke. They praised his humility, contrasting it with the typical arrogance of those in power.

__data__.text: Angada's humble speech is praised by the monkeys, who declare their eagerness to return to Sugriva. They affirm their obedience to Angada, stating they won't move without his command.  Angada agrees, and the powerful monkeys, led by Angada and Hanuman, leap into the air. They fill the sky like projectiles, roaring like a wind-driven storm.  Upon reaching Sugriva, Angada reassures a grieving Rama, confirming Sita's discovery and suggesting their success is evident in their timely return and Angada's joyful demeanor.  He reasons that Angada wouldn't approach Sugriva if they had failed, and even if he had, his demeanor would reflect defeat, which it clearly doesn't.  Sugriva adds that the monkeys wouldn't have dared destroy Madhuvana without finding Sita first.

__data__.text: Sugriva consoles Rama, assuring him that Sita has been found by Hanuman.  He emphasizes Hanuman's unique capabilities, intelligence, strength, and scholarship, highlighting that with leaders like Jambavan and Angada, and an advisor like Hanuman, the mission couldn't have failed.  Sugriva observes the monkeys' joyous return and the destruction of Madhuvana as further proof of their success. He hears their celebratory cries echoing their triumph.  Overjoyed, Sugriva stretches and curls his tail as Angada and Hanuman, along with the eager monkeys, approach Rama.  Angada and his company descend near Rama and Sugriva.  Hanuman respectfully informs Rama that Sita is safe and loyal.  Rama and Lakshmana are overjoyed by the news.  Lakshmana looks at the joyful Sugriva with respect and love, while Rama regards Hanuman with great affection and esteem.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman, along with other monkeys including Angada and Sugriva, respectfully approached Rama and Lakshmana to deliver news about Sita. They reported her confinement in Ravana's garden, guarded by ogresses, and relayed her unwavering devotion to Rama.  They also informed Rama of Ravana's ultimatum for Sita. Upon hearing that Sita was alive, Rama inquired about her current location and her feelings towards him.  The monkeys, acknowledging Rama's question, urged Hanuman, who had firsthand knowledge of Sita's situation, to provide a detailed account.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman, bowing respectfully towards the south where Sita was held captive, recounted to Rama how he found her. He described giving Sita Rama's ring and then shared details of her plight.  Sita was found in Ravana's garden, Lanka, closely guarded by monstrous ogresses.  She was grieving, wearing a single braid as a sign of mourning, sleeping on the bare ground, and refusing any comfort or association with Ravana.  Hanuman reassured her, gradually building her confidence by speaking of Rama's glory and his alliance with Sugriva.  Learning of this alliance brought Sita some joy. She remained utterly devoted to Rama.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman continued his report to Rama, explaining that Sita, deeply devoted to Rama and practicing asceticism, was found in Ravana's garden. She confirmed her identity by referencing the incident with the crow at Chitrakuta. Sita urged Hanuman to relay every detail of her plight to Rama.  She also gave him a jewel to return to Rama, reminding him of the mark he made on her forehead with realgar, and explaining that looking at this jewel brought her comfort.  Sita declared she could only endure another month of captivity.  Hanuman described Sita as emaciated and fearful, like a trapped doe.  Having delivered this message and the jewel, Hanuman concluded his report and urged Rama to devise a plan to cross the ocean.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}
