in many ways, Sri Rama urges Hanuman to repeat the message of Sita

Canto LXVI

Pressing to his bosom the jewel for the head sent by Sita and piteously wailing
in many ways, Sri Rama urges Hanuman to repeat the message of Sita

__data__.text: Overwhelmed with grief, Rama clutches Sita's jewel and weeps with Lakshmana.  He tells Sugriva that the jewel, a gift from Janaka to Sita, evokes the same longing as a cow feels for her calf. Rama explains the jewel's history, a gift from Indra to Janaka, and how seeing it is like seeing both his father-in-law and Sita.  He believes that the jewel's presence signifies Sita's survival.  Rama implores Hanuman to repeat Sita's message.  He laments to Lakshmana the pain of seeing the jewel without Sita and declares he cannot live without her.  Rama urges Hanuman to take him to where Sita is held captive, anxious about her safety among the fearsome Rakshasas.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Rama, holding Sita's jewel, continues to grieve. He remarks that Sita's face, usually bright like the autumn moon, is now dimmed, as if by clouds.  Desperate for news, he begs Hanuman to repeat Sita's message, saying it sustains him like medicine.  He asks again what Sita, his beloved wife, beautiful and sweet-spoken, said.  Separated from him and facing immense hardship, Rama wonders how Sita endures.

__data__.text: Hanuman recounts Sita's story to Rama. Sita told Rama about an incident at Chitrakuta.  While Rama slept, a crow pecked Sita's breast.  She awoke Rama, and the crow attacked again.  Seeing Sita's injury, Rama, enraged, questioned her.  He spotted the blood-stained crow, identified as Jayanta, son of Indra.  Rama, furious, took a blade of kusa grass and imbued it with Brahma's power to punish the crow.

__data__.text: Sita recounted to Hanuman how a crow had harassed her at Citrakuta.  She attacked the crow, which then begged Rama for mercy.  Despite being capable of destroying the crow, Rama, known for his compassion, only injured its eye.  Sita questioned why Rama, so skilled in combat, did not use his power against the demons who had abducted her. She lamented her fate, wondering if her own past actions were responsible for her current plight. She expressed her anguish that both Rama and Lakshmana, powerful warriors, seemed unable to protect her.  Hanuman, witnessing Sita's distress, reassured her that Rama was consumed by grief due to their separation, and that Lakshmana shared his brother's sorrow.  He emphasized that now, having found her, it was not the time for despair.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman tells Rama that Sita, despite her sorrow, expressed hope that Rama and Lakshmana would destroy Ravana and his army and rescue her.  Sita gave Hanuman a jewel to take back to Rama as a token of recognition.  As Hanuman prepared to depart, Sita, overwhelmed with grief, envied Hanuman's opportunity to see Rama and Lakshmana.  Hanuman offered to carry Sita back to Rama, but she refused, stating it would be improper for her to do so.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman recounts Sita's words to Rama, detailing her distress when Ravana abducted her. Despite her helplessness against Ravana's aggression, she urges Hanuman to return to Rama and Lakshmana.  She implores Hanuman to convey her well-being to the princes, Sugriva, and his ministers.  Sita asks Hanuman to deliver her message in a manner that will compel Rama to rescue her from her misery.  She also asks Hanuman to inform Rama of her profound sorrow and the threats she endures from the ogres.  Finally, Hanuman assures Rama that Sita, though distressed, remains resolute and steadfast in her devotion.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman recounted to Rama how Sita initially doubted a monkey's ability to cross the vast ocean.  Sita urged Hanuman to convey to Rama the urgency of her situation, suggesting he either persuade Rama to swiftly defeat Ravana and rescue her or, if necessary, rest and return the following day. Sita expressed fear for her safety upon Hanuman's departure, anticipating renewed grief.  She voiced her significant doubt about how any monkey, even with Hanuman's assistance, or even Rama and Lakshmana themselves, could possibly cross the formidable ocean.  Sita acknowledged the unique capability of only Garuda, the wind-god, and Hanuman to achieve such a feat.  She then implored Hanuman, whom she recognized as resourceful, to propose a solution, suggesting that while he was capable of rescuing her single-handedly, Rama's triumph over Ravana with his army would bring him greater glory.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman reassured Sita, explaining that Rama would not resort to the same deceitful tactics Ravana used.  A triumphant, open victory over Ravana and his forces in Lanka would be a more fitting way for Rama to reclaim her.  Hanuman emphasized that he was working towards such a glorious outcome. He highlighted Sugriva's commitment to Sita's rescue and the vast, powerful monkey army at their disposal. This army, he described, was incredibly strong, fast, and capable, having circumnavigated the earth. He pointed out that while there were monkeys as strong or stronger than himself, it was customary for the younger ones to be sent on missions like his. Hanuman urged Sita to abandon her despair, promising a swift arrival of the monkey army and envisioning Rama and Lakshmana, carried on his back, coming for her like the rising sun and moon.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman painted a vivid picture of Rama's imminent arrival for Sita. He described Rama, lion-like in his strength, arriving at Lanka's gates with Lakshmana.  He spoke of the monkey army, comparing their strength and fierceness to lions, tigers, and elephants, and their numbers to mountains and clouds, soon to fill Lanka with their roars from the Trikuta mountain.  He reassured Sita that she would witness Rama's coronation in Ayodhya upon their return from exile. Hanuman's comforting and encouraging words brought solace to Sita, easing her grief and despair.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto VII

The poet incidentally draws a pen-picture of Ravana’s palace and his
aerial car known by the name of Puspaka

Canto VII

The poet incidentally draws a pen-picture of Ravana’s palace and his
aerial car known by the name of Puspaka

__data__.text: Hanuman observed Ravana's palace, a magnificent structure adorned with gold and cat's-eye lattices, teeming with birds and resembling a massive cloud formation illuminated by lightning.  He noted various apartments filled with conches, bows, and other weapons, as well as spacious attics atop the towering mansions.  Hanuman saw opulent mansions, revered by gods and demons alike, flawless and acquired from Kubera. These mansions, strategically arranged and seemingly built by the renowned architect Maya, surpassed all others in excellence.  He then beheld Ravana's splendid palace, radiant like gold and a towering cloud, a fitting residence for the powerful king.  This palace, a celestial paradise on earth, glittered with countless jewels and blossoms, resembling a pollen-covered mountain peak.  Illuminated by the presence of women, it shone like a cloud lit by lightning, a magnificent aerial chariot soaring through the sky, drawn by majestic swans.  Within the palace, Hanuman saw an aerial car, adorned with precious stones and resembling a vibrant, multi-hued cloud.  It was reminiscent of a mountain peak, rich with minerals, under a starlit sky.  The platform for boarding the aerial car featured artificial hills of gems and gold, covered with artificial trees bearing artificial flowers, complete with filaments and petals.  White mansions, pools adorned with flowers, lotuses, and charming groves with lakes were also present. Hanuman observed the magnificent Puspaka, a vast aerial car named after flowers, capable of long journeys and radiating the brilliance of precious stones.  It was considered among the finest of aerial cars, fit for gods.  The car was decorated with birds crafted from cat's-eye gems, silver, and coral.  Serpents made of jewels and beautifully formed horses, resembling noble steeds, were also present.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

Canto VII

The poet incidentally draws a pen-picture of Ravana’s palace and his
aerial car known by the name of Puspaka

__data__.text: Hanuman examined the aerial car, Puspaka, noting the sculpted birds with beaks and wings adorned with coral and gold flowers.  These birds appeared to have playfully drawn in their wings and resembled companions of Cupid.  Within the car, artificial elephants with ornate trunks offered lotus petals to a statue of Lakshmi, the goddess of fortune.  Hanuman marveled at the car's splendor, comparing it to a beautiful mountain with caverns and a fragrant tree in springtime.  Despite being able to move freely within Ravana's well-guarded city, Hanuman was distressed at not finding Sita, the respected daughter of King Janaka.  Even with his disciplined mind and keen observation, he could not locate her.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto VIII
A further description of the aerial car, Puspaka

Canto VIII
A further description of the aerial car, Puspaka

__data__.text: Hanuman, son of the wind-god, discovered a magnificent aerial car within the palace.  This vehicle, adorned with gems and intricate gold latticework, was a marvel of craftsmanship.  It was a creation of Viswakarma, the celestial architect, and shone brilliantly as it rested in the sky. Every detail of the car reflected meticulous effort and was inlaid with precious stones.  Its unique features surpassed even those found in the abodes of the gods. Acquired by Ravana through dedicated asceticism and focus, the Puspaka car responded to its master's thoughts, traveling swiftly and remaining inaccessible to the unworthy. Its construction involved specific designs and materials gathered from various locations.  This remarkable vehicle was reserved for virtuous individuals of great merit and renown, offering them unparalleled comfort and speed. Its distinct form, adorned with numerous compartments, resembled a mountain peak with multiple summits, shining brightly like the autumnal moon.  Carried by a multitude of swift, powerful, and wide-eyed spirits who roamed the night sky, the Puspaka car, decorated with spring flowers, presented a captivating sight, surpassing even the beauty of the spring season itself.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman, searching for Sita, surveyed Ravana's opulent palace from the Puspaka vimana. The vast, heavily guarded compound housed numerous structures, including the main palace, which was filled with Ravana's wives and captive princesses.  The palace's splendor rivaled that of Kubera, Yama, and Varuna.  Within the complex, Hanuman observed another magnificent edifice,  the celestial chariot Puspaka, originally crafted for Brahma and later won by Ravana from Kubera.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman, from his vantage point on the magnificent Puspaka vimana, observed a grand palace within Ravana's compound.  This structure, gleaming with gold and silver pillars, was adorned with intricate carvings and resembled the celestial mountains Meru and Mandara.  Numerous chambers and pavilions, radiant like the sun and fire, surrounded the main building.  Viswakarma, the divine architect, had crafted this edifice with golden stairways and exquisite platforms of sapphire and emerald.  Lattice windows of gold and crystal added to its splendor. The floors, inlaid with coral, precious gems, and pearls, shimmered like the rising sun.  The air was filled with the fragrant aroma of sandalwood and other sweet scents, further enhancing the palace's allure. This captivating aroma seemed to beckon Hanuman closer.  He then discovered a lavish hall, Ravana's favorite, adorned with gem-studded stairways and gold latticework. Crystal floors inlaid with ivory, pearls, diamonds, coral, silver, and gold created a dazzling effect.  Tall, ornate pillars, resembling outstretched wings, gave the impression that the hall was about to ascend to heaven. A large carpet depicting mountains, trees, and rivers covered the floor, while murals showcased various lands and dwellings.  The sounds of exotic birds and the lingering scent of celestial perfumes filled the air.  This hall, used by Ravana, was decorated with tapestries and perfumed with incense.  It was a place of radiant beauty, captivating the senses and dispelling all sorrow. Like a nurturing mother, this hall offered delights to all of Hanuman's senses.  He wondered if he had stumbled upon paradise, the abode of the gods, or even the realm of Brahma, the ultimate achievement.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman observed the golden lamps within Ravana's chamber, their light seemingly dimmed by Ravana's own splendor, as if in quiet contemplation. The chamber glowed with the combined radiance of the lamps, Ravana's presence, and the gleam of ornaments.  Hanuman then saw numerous women reclining on a carpet, adorned in colorful garments and garlands. They lay asleep, overcome by drink and slumber after a night of revelry.  The sleeping women, bedecked in silent jewelry, resembled a serene lotus pond, with swans and bees resting peacefully. Their closed eyes and lips, fragrant like lotuses, seemed like blossoms that had opened at nightfall and closed again with the coming of dawn.  Hanuman likened their faces to lotuses, attracting the attention of admirers, much like flowers draw bees.  The chamber, illuminated by these women, shone like a clear autumn sky filled with stars.  Ravana, surrounded by them, appeared like the moon amidst the stars. Hanuman mused that these women might be the fallen stars, gathered together in this place.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Within Ravana's chamber, the sleeping women resembled a scattered constellation of stars. Their beauty and grace shone brightly, yet their finery was disarrayed from their revelries.  Loose hair, scattered jewels, and shifted garments marked the aftermath of their evening's pleasures. Some had smudged makeup, displaced anklets, and broken necklaces.  Their attire, including pearl necklaces and loosened girdles, lay askew, like tired horses after a long journey.  Discarded earrings and crushed garlands further contributed to the scene, resembling blossoms trampled by an elephant.  The remaining jewelry, including pearl necklaces and golden chains, glimmered against their skin, resembling swans or birds nestled on riverbanks.  The women’s adorned bodies, marked by the impressions of their ornaments, resembled streams, complete with golden lotuses and the subtle movements of their breath stirring the fabric draped over them.  These coverings, like colorful banners, highlighted the diverse beauty of Ravana's wives.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: The sleeping women continued to stir slightly. Their earrings swayed gently with the rhythm of their breaths.  The air was filled with the sweet scent of sugared wine on their breath, which seemed to linger around Ravana.  Some of the women, in their drunken stupor, mistakenly reached for their companions, believing they were reaching for Ravana.  Lost in their intoxication and desire for Ravana, they inadvertently found comfort in each other's embrace.  Some used their arms as pillows, others rested their heads on garments.  They lay intertwined, heads resting on bosoms, arms, laps, and breasts.  The scene resembled a tangled, fragrant vine, the women's limbs entwined like blossoming creepers in springtime, their hair and jewels like bees buzzing amongst the flowers.  So intermingled were they that it was impossible to distinguish whose jewelry, clothing, or limbs belonged to whom, even though each item remained distinctly visible.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: The lamps, like unwinking eyes, watched over Ravana's sleeping harem.  These women, diverse in their beauty, were the wives of Ravana, some having chosen him out of love or desire, others having become his through his conquests.  Among them were the daughters of royal sages, Brahmins, demons, Gandharvas, and ogres.  The verses emphasize that, with the exception of Sita, the daughter of Janaka, none of these women were taken by force.  They were won over by Ravana's prowess, valor, or good looks, and each was devoted to him.  Furthermore, each of these women was of noble birth, beautiful, graceful, adorned, strong, and pleasing to Ravana.  Hanuman, observing this scene, reflected on the irony of Sita's situation.  He thought that if Ravana had allowed Sita the same happiness and respect that he showed his other wives, his life would have been truly blessed.  Instead, Ravana had committed a grave and dishonorable act by abducting Sita, who was clearly superior to all of the women in Ravana's harem.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto I

Desirous of reaching Lanka in order to discover Sita, Hanuman takes a leap
from a peak of Mount Mahendra and honouring with the touch of his hand
Mount Mainaka, which rose from the bottom of the sea to provide rest
on its peak to Hanuman, encounters Surasa (mother of Nagas), sent
by the gods in the form of an ogress, who was waiting for Hanuman
with her mouth open to devour him. He enters her belly
assuming a minute form and comes out without killing her.
Further, he is met by another ogress, Simhika by name,
standing with her mouth wide open to gulp Hanuman.

He enters her mouth after assuming a minute form
and comes out after splitting her belly open and

killing her. Then contracting his body into a
minute form again in order to preclude
the attention of the ogres, he descends
on a mountain peak outside Lanka

on its peak to Hanuman, encounters Surasa (mother of Nagas), sent
by the gods in the form of an ogress, who was waiting for Hanuman
with her mouth open to devour him. He enters her belly
assuming a minute form and comes out without killing her.
Further, he is met by another ogress, Simhika by name,
standing with her mouth wide open to gulp Hanuman.

He enters her mouth after assuming a minute form
and comes out after splitting her belly open and

killing her. Then contracting his body into a
minute form again in order to preclude
the attention of the ogres, he descends
on a mountain peak outside Lanka

Canto I

Desirous of reaching Lanka in order to discover Sita, Hanuman takes a leap
from a peak of Mount Mahendra and honouring with the touch of his hand
Mount Mainaka, which rose from the bottom of the sea to provide rest
on its peak to Hanuman, encounters Surasa (mother of Nagas), sent
by the gods in the form of an ogress, who was waiting for Hanuman
with her mouth open to devour him. He enters her belly
assuming a minute form and comes out without killing her.
Further, he is met by another ogress, Simhika by name,
standing with her mouth wide open to gulp Hanuman.

He enters her mouth after assuming a minute form
and comes out after splitting her belly open and

killing her. Then contracting his body into a
minute form again in order to preclude
the attention of the ogres, he descends
on a mountain peak outside Lanka

Canto X

Hanuman catches sight of Ravana reposing on an excellent couch decked
with various ornaments, himself adorned with jewels of every description.
Perceiving Mandodari graced with bright jewels and lying asleep on a
wonderful couch not far from her husband in the midst of dancing
girls lying in a disorderly state with diverse musical instruments
clasped to their bosom, and taking her to be Sita, Hanuman
gets enraptured and exhibits his joy through various
simian gestures such as kissing the end of his tail

Canto XI
Banishing by recourse to reason the thought that the lady whom he had seen
was Sita, Hanuman searches for Sita once more in the gynaeceum as well
as in the banqueting hall and sees there a number of women lying
in various states, as well as drinking vessels. He is seized with
fear of having incurred the sin of gazing on others’
wives; but the thought that he had looked on them
with a lust-free mind eases his conscience

Canto XII
Having failed to discover Sita even after searching for her in the picture
gallery and other places, Hanuman suspects that she might have been
disposed of by Ravana and, thus meeting with frustration, falls into
the quagmire of despair. On second thought, however, he deprecates
despair as harmful and, falling back upon self-reliance,
resumes the search. But, failing to find her even on
searching for her all round, he becomes despondent again

Canto XIII
Coming out of the aerial car, Puspaka, and not finding Sita even on searching
for her on all sides, Hanuman concludes her to have been killed. Believing
that her untraceability, no matter whether it is reported to Srt Rama or not,
may lead to disastrous consequences, Hanuman decides not to return to
the mainland. Meanwhile, as he contemplates fasting till death or
suicide or the killing of Ravana, he catches sight of a grove
which he does not remember to have seen and before
proceeding to explore it mentally invokes the succour
of Rsis and gods for success in his undertaking

Canto XIV
Leaping down to the enclosure of the Asoka grove and watching the loveliness
of the grove from the top of the wall, Hanuman enters the grove and,
leaping from tree to tree in the course of his search for Sita, catches
sight of an Asoka tree. Espying a stream running beside it and
expecting that Sita might turn up on the bank of the stream to
say her Sandhya prayers, he takes up his position on that
Asoka tree, hiding himself behind its leafy boughs

Canto XXI
Placing a blade of grass between herself and Ravana in order to avoid
direct contact with a man of evil intentions and expostulating with
him by showing him the right path, Sita praises Sri Rama and,
impressing on Ravana the consequences of the latter
befriending or antagonizing Sri Rama, advises Ravana
to make friends with Sri Rama through self-surrender

Canto XXII

Nettled by the censure uttered by Sita, Ravana allows her a time-limit of two
months to revise her decision and threatens her with death if she does not
listen to reason. Restored to confidence by the glances of Ravana’s
consorts, however, Sita condemns him once more. Leaving instructions
with ogresses of terrible and ugly aspect to bring her to reason by
recourse to intimidation and persuasion, Ravana thereupon
leaves the presence of Sita alongwith his womenfolk

Canto XXIV
Citing the examples of Saci, Arundhati and other devoted wives, even
though threatened by the ogresses, Sita with a stout heart boldly
proclaims her resolve not to submit to anyone else other than
her own husband even on pain of death. Hanuman silently
watches her being menaced by the ogresses by means
of harsh words and show of weapons, and weeping

intimidating Sita, of a dream she saw only a few minutes before
revealing the triumph of Sri Rama and the discomfiture of Ravana,
and stops them from molesting Sita. Pressed by them,
she relates the dream to them and also speaks
of the omens portending the triumph of Sita

Sri Rama and ending with Hanuman’s espying Sita, narrated in a
human tongue by Hanuman, remaining perched on the Sirhéapa
tree and casting her eyes all round, Sita catches sight

Sirhgapa tree beneath which she stood

Canto XXXII
Imagining Hanuman to have been seen in a dream, though actually perceived
by her, and believing the sight of a monkey in a dream to be ominous,
Sita becomes anxious about the welfare of Sri Rama and others.
Then concluding him on other grounds to have been seen in
her waking hours, she prays to Brahma and other gods
that the statement of Hanuman may come out to be true

Canto XXXIV
Inferring from the suitable reply to his queries received from Sita and the
trust reposed in him by the latter, Hanuman reveals to her the role of an
envoy allotted to him by Sri Rama and repeat the inquiries made by
the two brothers about her welfare. Taking him to be Ravana
disguised as a monkey, Sita, on the other hand, reproaches him
when the latter approaches her in order to bow down at her
feet. In order to allay her fears, Hanuman for his part

discloses to her his reality as a minister of Sugriva as

well as requests her to give credence to his words

when the latter approaches her in order to bow down at her
feet. In order to allay her fears, Hanuman for his part

discloses to her his reality as a minister of Sugriva as

well as requests her to give credence to his words

Canto XXXVI
Hanuman delivers Sri Rama’s signet ring to Sita in order to strengthen her
confidence in him. Applauding Hanuman, Sita, who was rejoiced to
receive the token, inquires about the health of Sri Rama and others.
In order to appease the lady, who felt indignant at the thought that
S17 Rama did not care to come to her rescue so far, which led
her to suspect that he had ceased to love her, Hanuman
attributes his non-appearance to his ignorance about her
whereabouts. Describing Sri Rama’s desolation in order
to betoken his excessive love for her, he consoles
her with the assurance that Sri Rama is sparing
no pains to rescue her from her confinement

Canto XXXVIII
Asked by Hanuman for a token, Sita narrates the episode of a crow,
which occurred on the Citrakita mountain, and asks Hanuman to
repeat the story to Sri Rama by way of a token. She further sends
her greetings both to Srt Rama and Laksmana, conveys her
inquiry after their welfare and communicates to them her
resolve to survive only for a month after the expiry of the
time-limit prescribed by Ravana; and last of all, she
hands over to him her jewel for the head as a token

Canto XXXIX
Sita asks Hanuman, when the latter is about to depart with the jewel for her
head, to apprise Sri Rama and Laksmana of her own welfare and urge them
to rescue her. When Sita expresses her misgivings about the capacity of
the heroes on Sri Rama’s side to cross the sea, Hanuman expatiates
on the might of the monkey heroes and assures her of their ability
to cross it, and in the end consoles her by promising to bring
Sri Rama and Laksmana on his back across the sea in any case

Canto XL
Asking Hanuman, who was ready to depart, to remind Sri Rama of his
having once painted a decorative mark on her cheek with red realgar,
and also of how he threw a reed at the crow assailing her, destroying
his right eye thereby, as further tokens of Hanuman having met
her, and urging him to apprise Sri Rama of her wretched
plight, Sita grants him leave to depart with her blessings

Canto XLI
Accounting his purpose not fully accomplished till he had ascertained the
strength of the ogres, even though he had received the message of
Sita, and making up his mind to lay waste the royal
pleasure-garden as a means of ascertaining
the strength of the enemy, Hanuman
proceeds to do it

Canto XLII
Perceiving the devastation of the royal pleasance at the hands of Hanuman,
the ogresses keeping watch over Sita asked her who he was. On Sita’s
pleading her ignorance in the matter, some of them hurriedly seek
the presence of Ravana and report the matter to him. Hanuman
makes short work of a company of ogres known by
the name of Kinkaras, despatched by Ravana.
Thereupon Ravana sends Prahasta’s
son to punish the intruder

Canto XLIII
Having disposed of the Kinkaras sent by Ravana and making up
his mind to demolish the sanctuary sacred to the guardian deity
of ogres, Hanuman climbs it up and, killing the guards posted
there, exhibits his prowess by uttering the name of Sri
Rama in a thundering voice. Nay, uprooting a pillar
of the sanctuary, he brandishes it and burns the
sanctuary with the fire produced thereby

Canto XLVIII
Dispatched by Ravana, Indrajit, Ravana’s eldest son, marches against
Hanuman. On his shafts being rendered ineffective by Hanuman
through his extreme agility, Indrajit takes him captive by discharging
the unfailing missile presided over by Brahma (the creator).
Even though capable of rendering it ineffective, Hanuman
yields to its influence, eager as he was to meet
Ravana, to whose presence he is led by his son

Canto V
Even though seeing ogres and ogresses of various grades and orders while

the moon was ascending the heavens, Hanuman gives way to
anxiety on his not being able to find Sita

Canto L
Commanded by Ravana, Prahasta inquires of Hanuman as to who he was as
also his motive in devastating the royal pleasance and killing the ogres.
In reply Hanuman says that he destroyed the grove in order that he
might be taken captive and dragged to the presence of Ravana,
whom he was eager to see, and was compelled in self-defence to
kill those who stood in his way. In the end he declares himself
to be a messenger of Sri Rama and adds that, though protected

against the missile presided over by Brahma, he submitted

to its influence only in order to be able to see Ravana

Canto LI
Narrating the story of Sri Rama from the latter’s entry into the forest, to his
own sight of Sita being borne away by Ravana through the air over the
Rsyamika mountain, and celebrating the glory of Sri Rama, Hanuman
points out to Ravana that if he longed to survive he should
restore Sita to Sri Rama and that he should be prepared for the
worst if, on the other hand, he chooses not to part with her

Canto LII
Provoked by the harsh words of Hanuman, Ravana orders him to be put
to death. Vibhisana, however, exhorts the latter to desist from
this dastardly act, pointing out that the killing of an
envoy is forbidden by the Sastras

Canto LIII
Wrapping up the tail of Hanuman in rags and soaking it in oil, and then
lighting it up, the ogres, as urged by Ravana, take Hanuman round the city
to the accompaniment of beat of drums. Hearing this news from the lips
of the ogresses, Sita prays to the god of fire on oath to see that
Hanuman remains unscathed. Climbing up the gate and shedding
the fire by attenuating his body, and then resuming vast

proportions and seizing the iron bar of the gate,

Hanuman kills with it the ogres guarding the gate

Canto LIV
Making up his mind to set fire to the city of Lanka, which was the only
work left for him to do, and coming out into the open, Hanuman
burns the entire city barring the abode of Vibhisana.
Astonished to see the city burning, the gods and
Gandharvas (celestial musicians) are
filled with supreme joy

his journey to Lanka, reached Lanka, beheld Janaka’s
daughter and returned to Mount Mahendra

Canto LIX
Picturing to his fellow monkeys the said plight of Sita, and feeling that
it behoved them all to see Sri Rama only after recovering Sita
from Lanka, nay, recounting the past heroic exploits of
Jambavan and others, Hanuman incites them all to
have recourse to the arbitrament of war

Canto LXI
Set out from the Mahendra mountain in the direction of Kiskindha, the
monkeys halt at Madhuvana, a grove guarded by the monkey Dadhimukha,
and most beloved of Sugriva, which falls on their way. When the
monkeys began to enjoy the fruits of the grove with the
permission of Prince Angada, Dadhimukha, comes in
their way and is lacerated with their claws and teeth

Canto LXI
Set out from the Mahendra mountain in the direction of Kiskindha, the
monkeys halt at Madhuvana, a grove guarded by the monkey Dadhimukha,
and most beloved of Sugriva, which falls on their way. When the
monkeys began to enjoy the fruits of the grove with the
permission of Prince Angada, Dadhimukha, comes in
their way and is lacerated with their claws and teeth

Canto LXII
Having entered Madhuvana and partaken of honey, as permitted by Hanuman,
and singing and dancing like drunken people, the monkeys were forbidden
by those guarding the grove and they belaboured the latter. Repulsed by
them, the guards reported the matter to their leader, Dadhimukha,
who appeared on the scene with his entourage and on remonstrating
with the monkeys was thrashed by Angada, who dashed
him to the ground. The guards headed by Dadhimukha
then move en masse to Kiskindha to report the
matter to Sugriva and bow down at the latter’s feet

Canto LXIII
Having heard from the mouth of Dadhimukha the story of Madhuvana
having been laid waste by the monkeys, Sugriva concludes from
the jubilation of the monkeys that Sita has been traced out.
Comforting Dadhimukha, he asks him to send Angada
and others without delay

Canto LXIV
Returning to Madhuvana as commanded by Sugriva, and craving the
forgiveness of Angada, Dadhimukha submits to the latter that
he is urgently summoned by Sugriva. Set out in the
company of Hanuman and others, and approaching
Sugriva, Prince Angada apprises Sit Rama, after
saluting him, of the discovery of Sita
and other events

Canto LXVI

Pressing to his bosom the jewel for the head sent by Sita and piteously wailing
in many ways, Sri Rama urges Hanuman to repeat the message of Sita

Canto LXVII
Hanuman reproduces at length the incident connected with a crow that
took place during the sojourn of Sita with Sri Rama at Citrakiita,
related by her by way of a token, and further describes her
piteous lament and the way in which she was
comforted by Hanuman
